Want to Develop With Claude Code? This Guide Takes You From Beginner to Advanced

As a developer, you've probably heard of AI Agent dev tools like Gemini, Codex, Claude Code, and so on. Most developers these days subscribe to a few AI Agent tools to help speed up dev workflows. This post picks one AI Agent tool — Claude Code — and walks through how to use it for development. There may be acceleration techniques and usage concepts here you don't know yet, and this post includes practical examples so you can get up to speed faster.

As always, let's first understand what Claude Code is. Claude Code is an AI Agent tool developed by Anthropic, primarily based on Large Language Models (LLM) to help developers with code generation, debugging, and code optimization. The design goal of Claude Code is to let developers more efficiently complete various programming tasks — whether writing new code or maintaining existing code, it provides solid support.
Anthropic has many products, for example:
: basically another ChatGPT — primarily for conversational AI interaction.

: AI Agent tool designed for developers — helps with code-related tasks. The star of this post. Beyond the terminal, there are also Desktop App, Web App (claude.ai/code), VS Code, and JetBrains extensions.
: in short, you can use Cowork to manage your computer — let it organize your desktop, write documents, handle daily tasks.
: a visual design tool released in April 2026 — can build visual mockups, prototypes, presentations, and pass designs to Claude Code for implementation.
There are also some well-known features that aren't products per se but more like features or protocols:
: a browser extension that lets users use Claude features directly in the browser.
: lets users interact with Claude directly in Slack workspaces to help with various work tasks.
: yes, this lets Excel users use Claude for data analysis, report generation, and so on.
: MCP is a standard proposed by Anthropic — basically a standardized protocol that lets legacy systems (or systems that can't interact with AI) interact with other AI systems through MCP.

: you could say this was designed to solve MCP causing Context bloat — lets developers build dedicated skills for specific tasks, with the AI calling these skills based on task needs. This helps reduce Context usage, since Tokens are expensive.
After getting acquainted with Anthropic, let's understand how to use Claude Code for development — Claude Code is the star of this post.
There are quite a few install methods. The currently recommended one is the Native Installer — no need to install Node.js separately, and supports auto-update:
curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.cmd -o install.cmd && install.cmd && del install.cmd
Homebrew offers two casks, and the difference is which update channel they track:

# Stable channel: skips releases with major regressions, but usually about a week behind the latest
brew install --cask claude-code

# Latest channel: gets new versions the moment they ship
brew install --cask claude-code@latest
# Stable channel: skips releases with major regressions, but usually about a week behind the latest
# Latest channel: gets new versions the moment they ship
Whichever cask you pick, Homebrew won't auto-update — you'll need to run
yourself (depending on which one you installed) to update.
So which one should you pick?
(stable) skips releases with major regressions, at the cost of getting new features about a week late.

stays on the bleeding edge. The risk with the latter is you might hit a fresh bug that just shipped and hasn't been reported yet — basically you're the guinea pig for Anthropic. Personally I switched to
because I want to play with new stuff like the Workflows feature below the moment it lands, but you've got to accept the occasional landmine. If you'd rather stay safe, just use the
Pick the install method that fits your needs. Once installed, you can start using Claude Code for development.
to launch the Claude Code interactive interface:
If it's your first time, you'll be asked to log in. If not, you can also type
When logging in, you have two account options:
Claude Console: basically API-based — relatively pricier since it's billed by usage, similar to OpenAI's API.
What's the difference between the two?
Subscription: gives you a usage cap. Current plans:
: $100/month, 5x the usage of Pro — for heavy users.
: $200/month, 20x the usage of Pro — for developers needing extended heavy use.
When you exceed the cap, you can't use it temporarily — wait for the quota to refresh or upgrade your plan.

API usage billing: billed by actual Tokens used. Since there's no cap, you can use Claude Code without limit. Downside: the price is relatively higher. Usually for enterprises or developers who need heavy usage.
Claude Code requires Pro or higher — the free Claude.ai account can't use Claude Code.
So assuming you've already entered the project folder where you want to use Claude Code and logged in, let's start using Claude Code for development.

Next let's get familiar with the "memory file (CLAUDE.md)" produced after running

. This file is really important — it determines how Claude Code performs during development, because it tells Claude Code about the project's structure, language, framework, and even which docs to reference during dev.
The CLAUDE.md file has multiple types — each location has a different purpose:
): your personal preferences — always use Traditional Chinese, prefer some programming language or framework, particular dev style, etc. All projects on your computer share this personal memory.
): rules document for this project — the company's internal coding standards, dev process, team conventions, etc.
): things like specific language guides, framework usage standards, particular dev rules — making Claude Code's dev work better fit project needs.
): special dev preferences for this file that you don't want to share with other devs — usually personal preferences, so it's added to
to avoid getting pushed to remote.
Why do we need these files? The purpose is to let Claude Code, after initialization (typing
in the project), understand the project's structure, language, framework, and which docs to reference during dev.
How do we know CLAUDE.md is being correctly read? When you type
, if you see a prompt asking you to type
, that means Claude Code didn't read a CLAUDE.md file. So you need to type
to have Claude Code initialize for this project.
From my testing, I'd recommend placing it at

CLAUDE.md, in plain English, is a project README.md. When a new person joins, you wouldn't make them re-learn the project from scratch every time — you need a doc to help newcomers quickly understand the project's structure, language, framework, etc., so they can pick it up faster.
A standard project memory CLAUDE.md should contain at minimum:

generates. To avoid CLAUDE.md becoming overly long, I'd suggest splitting content into multiple files and referencing them from CLAUDE.md — making Claude Code more efficient at finding docs.
For each referenced doc, you can write so-called YAML frontmatter to help Claude Code understand the doc's purpose:

---
description: This is the project structure document for this project. When the user asks for a project structure explanation or wants to do project structure-related tasks, please reference this document's contents.
---
# Project structure

...

This is the project structure document for this project. When the user asks for a project structure explanation or wants to do project structure-related tasks, please reference this document's contents.

MCP stands for "Model Context Protocol" — a standardized protocol that lets systems unable to directly interact with AI to interact with other AI systems via MCP.
Since I've covered this in detail in earlier articles, I'd recommend reading these directly:
Heard of LLM, RAG, Prompt? A quick guide to common Generative AI terms
GitHub Copilot + MCP Server in VSCode: Build Your AI Dev Workflow
Build Your Own MCP Server for AI Agents — A Complete Implementation Guide With GitHub Copilot Chat
post — it has good intro coverage of various AI terms.
The MCP Server I'd strongly recommend using during development is

. The advantage of this open source MCP Server is that it lets your AI fetch real-time data. Why is that needed? AI is trained on data — when its training data is outdated, the results aren't accurate. So you need an MCP Server that lets AI fetch real-time data, improving the accuracy of AI's output.
Context specifically collects the latest official dev docs and real, usable code samples — basically a standard config for AI Agents.
How do you add it? The easiest way right now is the official one-click install command:
This handles OAuth verification, generates an API Key, and installs the corresponding MCP Server config automatically.
If you prefer manual setup, you can first apply for a free API Key at
(no credit card needed), then choose one of two ways to add the MCP Server:
Local mode: add MCP Server through Claude Code commands:
claude mcp add --scope user context7 -- npx -y @upstash/context7-mcp --api-key YOUR_API_KEY
Remote mode: configure the MCP Server through the Context 7 official site, then Claude Code will auto-fetch from it:
claude mcp add --scope user --header "CONTEXT7_API_KEY: YOUR_API_KEY" --transport http context7 https://mcp.context7.com/mcp
You can use Context7 without an API Key, but you'll have a lower rate limit (free accounts get about 1,000 requests/month). I'd recommend applying for a free API Key for higher usage.

What's the difference between remote and local? In short, in local mode the backend code runs on your computer and fetches from the remote DB. In remote mode, even the backend code runs remotely — the upside is you don't need to install anything locally.
Which is recommended? Both are fine — pick what fits your usage habits.
Beyond the above, you can also customize Claude Commands to better fit your needs. I'd recommend two I personally find quite useful:
: for running tests. Have Claude Code write test cases or fix failing tests — significantly boosts your testing efficiency.
: for code review. Have Claude Code check code quality, style, or find potential issues — ensures your code quality.
How to create these? Easy — just create a
folder in the project root and put two Markdown files inside, named

# Code testing command

Please run the following testing and quality assurance flow. Your goal is to ensure code correctness and robustness.

## Steps
1. **Command analysis**: check the `package.json` script definitions below to confirm the correct test command (e.g., `test` or `test:coverage`).
2. **Run tests**: use the Bash tool to execute the test command.
3. **Fix errors (if tests fail)**:
   - Analyze the stack trace.
   - **Don't simply modify the test to make it pass** unless you're sure the test case itself is wrong.
   - Fix the original code logic to pass the test.
4. **Add tests (if missing)**:
   - If the project lacks tests or coverage is insufficient, write unit tests for core business logic.
   - Ensure tests are **atomic** — don't depend on external environment (use Mock/Stub).

## Reference info (Context)
To help you pick the right command, here are the project's script settings:

`package.json` Scripts:
!`grep "scripts" -A 15 package.json`

# Code review command

Please conduct a **strict and objective technical review** of the following code changes — directly point out problems and provide concrete suggestions, no need for pleasantries.

## Review criteria
1. **Readability and conventions**: are names accurate? Is logic easy to understand? Does it conform to the language's idiomatic style?
2. **Security**: any risks of injection attacks, data leaks, or improper error handling?
3. **Performance and resources**: any unnecessary computation, memory leaks, or N+1 query problems?
4. **Code Smells**: detect duplicated logic, overly complex conditionals, or excessively long functions.

## Changes
Below are the results of `git status` and `git diff HEAD`:

File status:
!`git status -s`

Detailed changes:
!`git diff HEAD`
Review code changes in the current working directory (including Staged and Unstaged)
of the following code changes — directly point out problems and provide concrete suggestions, no need for pleasantries.
: are names accurate? Is logic easy to understand? Does it conform to the language's idiomatic style?
: any risks of injection attacks, data leaks, or improper error handling?
: any unnecessary computation, memory leaks, or N+1 query problems?
: detect duplicated logic, overly complex conditionals, or excessively long functions.

In these Markdown files, you'll see a few special YAML properties:
: lets users know what this Command is for — shown in Claude Code's command list.
: limits which tools this Command can use — avoids the Command using unnecessary tools and creating security issues.

Because MCP consumes too much Context, Anthropic launched Skills — letting devs build dedicated skills for specific tasks, with the AI invoking these skills based on task needs. This helps reduce Context usage, since Tokens are expensive.
I've covered this in detail in an earlier post, so I won't repeat it. Recommended reading:
Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP — Demoed With Claude Code
Yes — Claude Code also supports Hooks, letting you trigger custom behavior on specific events. This makes Claude Code more flexible and able to operate based on your needs.
For example, before each code commit, you can have Claude Code check code quality, raising your code quality.
The Hook events Claude Code supports (as of April 2026, 28 events total):
: triggered when a Claude Code session starts.
: triggered when a Claude Code session ends.
: triggered when a Claude Code turn ends.
: triggered when a turn ends due to API error.
: triggered when you submit a Prompt, before Claude Code processes it.
: triggered when a Slash Command expands into a Prompt.
: triggered when Claude Code emits a notification.
: triggered before a tool is used.
: triggered after a tool call succeeds.
: triggered after a tool call fails.
: triggered when Claude Code requests permission.
: triggered when Auto Mode's classifier denies an operation.
: triggered when an Agent Team teammate is about to go idle.
: triggered when a task is created.
: triggered when a task is marked complete.
: triggered when CLAUDE.md or rules files are loaded.
: triggered when config files change during the session.
: triggered when the working directory changes.
: triggered when monitored files change on disk.
: triggered when a Worktree is created.
: triggered when a Worktree is removed.
: triggered when an MCP Server requests user input during a tool call.
: triggered after the user responds to MCP Elicitation.
Why introduce this? Few people use it, but Hooks are really useful in certain situations:
After completing a specific task, run tslint or prettier to format the code — perfect for the
After Claude Code finishes a task, notify relevant people via Slack, Discord, etc. — use the
When the user submits a Prompt, do some preprocessing like filtering sensitive words — use the
When Claude Code wants to run
, do a double confirmation to avoid accidental deletion — use the
When Claude Code wants to read sensitive info like .env, AWS credentials, etc., encrypt first — use the
Before creating or editing files, ask Claude Code to do code review for code quality — use the
What's the format? This involves placement again:
): personal Hook config — affects Hook behavior across all projects on your computer.
): Hook config for this project — affects Hook behavior for this project, can be uploaded to GitHub for shared use with other devs.
): private Hook config for this project — usually personal preferences, so add to

Now let me introduce some Hook examples commonly used in development, so you can pick up Claude Code's Hook feature faster.
After Claude Code edits a file, auto-run Prettier to format the code:
After editing files, auto-run ESLint fix:
Auto Git Commit before edit (create backup point)
Claude Code modifies a file, create a backup commit so you can roll back later:
"git add -A && git commit -m 'backup: before Claude edit' --allow-empty || true"
When Claude Code stops, auto-commit all changes:
"git add -A && git commit -m 'feat: Claude Code auto-commit' || true"

#!/bin/bash
INPUT=$(cat)
COMMAND=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')

# Intercept dangerous commands
if [[ "$COMMAND" == *"rm -rf"* ]] || [[ "$COMMAND" == *"rm -r /"* ]]; then
  echo "Dangerous command blocked: $COMMAND" >&2
  exit 2  # exit 2 = block operation
fi

exit 0
If you want to do format, Lint, and TypeScript check all at once, write it as a script:

#!/bin/bash
INPUT=$(cat)
FILE_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')

# Only handle JS/TS files
if [[ ! "$FILE_PATH" =~ \.(js|jsx|ts|tsx)$ ]]; then
  exit 0
fi

# 1. Prettier format
npx prettier --write "$FILE_PATH" 2>/dev/null

# 2. ESLint fix
npx eslint --fix "$FILE_PATH" 2>/dev/null

# 3. TypeScript type check
npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | head -20

exit 0

Uses AI agent for multi-tool verification

I've covered a lot, but haven't introduced one thing yet — Mode.
In Claude Code, Mode defines Claude Code's behavior when executing tasks. Different Modes affect how Claude Code handles commands, uses tools, and interacts with users.
Claude Code currently supports 6 Permission Modes:
: the default mode. Claude Code asks for confirmation before file edits or shell commands — every operation is under user control.
Best for: first-time use, important projects, when you need careful review of every change.
: a read-only mode. Claude Code can only explore code and make plans — can't modify files.
Best for: analyzing unfamiliar codebases, planning large refactors, understanding scope of impact before action.
: auto-accepts file edits but still confirms shell commands.
Best for: rapid iteration, lots of file modifications, trust Claude Code's edits but still want to control system commands.
(added March 2026): all operations auto-execute, but a background AI safety classifier (Sonnet 4.6) reviews each operation, auto-blocking dangerous behavior. Currently in Research Preview.
Best for: fully automated dev flow with safety guardrails.
Limitation: requires Max, Team, Enterprise, or API plan (Pro doesn't support).
: only allows pre-approved tools to execute — all unauthorized operations are auto-denied.
Best for: CI/CD environments, automated scripts — strict tool usage scope control.
: skips all permission checks (except protected paths) — all operations execute directly.
Best for: only in isolated containers or VMs — strongly not recommended on local dev environments.

to switch between Default → Auto-accept Edits → Plan — the three commonly used modes. If your account plan supports Auto Mode, it appears in the cycle too. Other modes need to be started via the CLI parameter

For complex tasks, you may want Claude Code to think more deeply. Claude Code provides Extended Thinking — letting you trigger and view its reasoning process.
After Claude Code finishes a response, the thinking process shows in gray italic text in the terminal. To review previous thinking, use these shortcuts:

requesting an "always show thinking process" feature.

is recommended for the most complete thinking process.
Setting thinking budget via environment variable
You can also set the thinking Token budget via environment variable:
Higher values give Claude Code more "thinking space" for reasoning. Minimum budget is 1,024 tokens — start from minimum and increase as needed.
: when tasks involve multi-step reasoning, architecture design, or complex debug, Extended Thinking lets Claude Code analyze problems more comprehensively.
: more thinking time usually means more accurate answers.
: you can see how Claude Code reaches conclusions step by step.
When using Claude Code, Context management is really important — Context bloat consumes Tokens fast and even affects Claude Code's response quality.
Context is all the info Claude Code references when handling tasks, including:
: code or docs Claude Code read during the conversation
: if MCP is enabled, related response data also counts
These accumulate in memory so Claude Code can understand the conversation's context. Because of this, Context grows continuously as the conversation progresses.
When you see these, Context might be overloaded:
Claude Code starts "forgetting" previously discussed content
Response quality drops noticeably — repetitive or contradictory content
: compresses current conversation history, keeps important info but reduces Token usage. Use periodically after long conversations.
: if starting a brand new task, just clear conversation history and start over.
: if you need to review previous conversation, use this to load history.
: instead of having Claude Code handle an entire large refactor at once, split into multiple small tasks. After each completes, use
: avoid vague descriptions — precise Prompts reduce back-and-forth, which reduces Context accumulation.
anytime to check current Token status, helping you decide whether to do Context management.
Note this mode is only available for subscriptions.

Beyond interactive conversation mode, Claude Code also supports Headless Mode (non-interactive), letting you execute single tasks directly via command line — really useful for automation scripts or CI/CD integration.
claude -p "Please check whether the package.json in this project has outdated packages"
"Please check whether the package.json in this project has outdated packages"
After execution, Claude Code outputs the result and auto-exits — doesn't enter interactive mode.
You can also pipe content to Claude Code:
cat error.log | claude -p "Please analyze this error log and find the cause"
"Please analyze this error log and find the cause"
Or pipe git diff for code review:
git diff HEAD~1 | claude -p "Please review these code changes and point out potential issues"
"Please review these code changes and point out potential issues"
Headless Mode is great for CI/CD pipeline integration:

# GitHub Actions example
- name: Code Review with Claude
  run: |
    git diff ${{ github.event.before }} ${{ github.sha }} | claude -p "Review these changes. If serious issues, return 'FAIL', otherwise 'PASS'"
git diff ${{ github.event.before }} ${{ github.sha }} | claude -p "Review these changes. If serious issues, return 'FAIL', otherwise 'PASS'"
Execute single task and output result

Since this post was originally written in late January 2026, here are some important features added later so everyone can keep up with the latest Claude Code developments.
On April 14, 2026, Anthropic significantly redesigned the Claude Code Desktop App (Mac/Windows), adding:
Sidebar for managing multiple parallel sessions
If you prefer GUI, this Desktop App is a good choice.
Beyond terminal and desktop, you can now use Claude Code directly in the browser at
. The Web App can connect to your GitHub Repo and execute tasks in an isolated sandbox, auto-creating a PR when done. Available for Pro and Max subscribers.
Released April 14, 2026, Cloud Routines lets you set up automated tasks running in Anthropic's cloud environment. Three trigger types:
: auto-execute on PR creation, Push, Issue creation, etc.
Usage limits vary by plan: Pro 5/day, Max 15/day.
to create and manage these scheduled tasks.
If you're worried Claude Code modifying your working directory will affect ongoing development, use Worktree isolation mode:
This creates an independent Git Worktree, letting Claude Code modify in isolation — completely without affecting your current branch. Can also be paired with
so each Worktree runs in an independent tmux session.

Permission Mode added March 24, 2026 — covered in the Permission Modes section, won't repeat here. In short, Claude Code auto-executes all operations with a background AI safety classifier intercepting dangerous behavior. Requires Max plan or above.
This is probably the most notable update of late May 2026 — "Workflows" (dynamic workflows), shipped alongside Claude Opus 4.8.

Before this, if you wanted Claude Code to fire off a bunch of subagents at once, it had to decide the next step turn by turn on its own, and the intermediate results from all those agents got stuffed into the main conversation's Context. Once the task got big, things blew up easily.
Workflows works differently: it writes the orchestration logic — how many agents to spawn, who goes first, who verifies whom — into a script and runs it in the background. A few key points:
: the workflow runs in an isolated environment, so it doesn't block your current conversation — you can keep doing other things.
: it can run multiple subagents at once (roughly 16 at a time), and a single run can spin up dozens or even hundreds of agents over its lifetime.
: intermediate results live in script variables instead of getting dumped back into the main conversation like regular subagents do.
: a common pattern is having several agents independently do the same thing, then verify each other's results — which helps cut down on AI hallucination.
So how do you kick one off? Because this thing can spin up dozens of agents and burn a lot of Token, it won't run on its own by default — you have to explicitly ask for it:
keyword to your Prompt, for example:
ultracode: scan every API endpoint under src/routes/ and check for missing permission validation
Or just say it in plain language — "use a workflow to..." or "fan out multiple agents to..." — and Claude Code will decide whether to orchestrate one for you.
You can also use the built-in
, which is itself a workflow that fans out a bunch of web searches and then cross-checks the sources.
from earlier aren't the same thing.
gives Claude more thinking budget for this one turn;
asks it to handle the whole task with a multi-agent workflow. Totally different purposes — don't mix them up.
Once it's running, you can use the

command to watch the show — it lists running and completed workflows, showing each phase, how many agents got spawned, how much Token was spent, and how long it ran. You can also drill into a single agent's details, or just pause or stop the whole thing.
So when should you reach for a workflow? The official guidance is roughly these "too big to fit in one Context" scenarios:
: e.g. scanning the whole project for a certain class of bug or security hole.
: like a framework or package upgrade across hundreds of files.
Research or planning that needs multiple angles
: investigating the same question from several directions, letting them poke holes in each other, then converging on a conclusion.
On the flip side, if it's just a few small tasks, a regular subagent is fine — no need to go through the whole workflow song and dance, since it really does burn Token.
This feature requires a paid plan, and Pro users may need to turn on "Dynamic workflows" manually in
Since January 2026, Anthropic has released these new models:
Shipped with Workflows, same price as before, cheaper fast mode
The newest and most capable public model right now
parameter. Unless you've got a special need, the default model is plenty for most situations.
What's so great about Claude Opus 4.8?

The star this time is Claude Opus 4.8, released on May 28, 2026 — and it's also the default flagship model Claude Code uses now. Honestly, even Anthropic's own framing is pretty restrained: "a modest but tangible improvement over the previous generation." Still, a few points are genuinely useful for developers:
: same as 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 — $5 per million input tokens and $25 per million output tokens. So you're basically getting a better engine for the same money.
mode from the cheat sheet earlier runs about 2.5× faster on 4.8, and it now costs three times less than on older models — a lot lighter on the wallet when you want speed.
Less prone to making things up

: on "factual hallucination" benchmarks, 4.8 has the lowest wrong-answer rate among its peer models — and it gets there in an interesting way. Instead of forcing answers to more questions, it just says "I'm not sure" when it doesn't know, rather than fabricating something. For coding, that's actually more reliable.
: it shows clear gains on agent benchmarks like browser operation and long-running tasks — which is exactly why it can hold up the Workflows multi-agent orchestration from earlier.
In short, if you're already on Claude Code, this update is more like "same money, sturdier engine" — not much reason not to bump up to it.
Some issues I've personally hit while using Claude Code, with solutions for reference.
Can't switch Node.js versions with nvm after installing Claude Code
This was a particular situation when I installed Claude Code. I tried to recreate it but couldn't. To make sure other readers don't hit the same problem, I'm writing it up.
I installed via Homebrew, then suddenly couldn't switch Node.js versions with
. Investigation showed a global Node.js had been installed and overrode everything, breaking nvm.
The fix is simple. First, remove the Homebrew-installed Claude Code:
ignores dependencies, ensuring only Node.js gets removed without affecting other packages.
By this point, your nvm should work normally.
If you hit this issue from installing the curl version of Claude Code, you can try:
"installMethod is native, but claude command not found at /Users/users/.local/bin/claude"
This is because Claude Code calls and uses the executable at
, but sometimes due to install method or system environment, that path's executable isn't found.
First, check where Claude Code is currently installed:
For me, since I installed via Homebrew, it shows:
That path is correct, but the "
installMethod is native, but claude command not found at /Users/users/.local/bin/claude
" error still shows. This means Claude Code's initialized path got stuck. Just run:
folder, then restart Claude Code and re-run initialization and login — the error should be gone.

As a developer, you've probably heard of AI Agent dev tools like Gemini, Codex, Claude Code, and so on. Most developers these days subscribe to a few AI Agent tools to speed up their dev workflow. This post picks one AI Agent tool — Claude Code — and walks through how to use it for development. There may be acceleration techniques and usage concepts here you don't know yet, and this post includes practical examples so you can get up to speed faster.
